text: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are ubiquitous contaminants that have been shown to cause adverse health effects in humans and wildlife at ultratrace concentrations (ng·L−1) in the environment. Their removal and characterization at these low concentrations is not currently possible using on-site or field-deployable remediation technologies due to their extremely low detection limits. Moreover, traditional laboratory techniques for analyzing PFAS, including liquid and gas chromatography and mass spectrometry, require sophisticated and expensive instrumentation and therefore make the monitoring of PFAS in large distributed water systems impractical. In this study, we synthesized the amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) that selectively detect perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) and perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) aqueous solutions in the ng·L−1 concentration range. The AFPs are highly fluorinated at their emission sites and contain a poly(p-phenylene ethynylene)/polyfluorene main chain with strategically placed pyridine functional groups that act as binding sites toward the protonizable PFAS (acidic) via a proton-transfer reaction. The highly fluorinated segment of the polymer confines and concentrates the detected PFAS within the polymer matrix, while the protonated sites of the pyridine act as the exciton traps. Upon binding of the target analyte, the resulting pyridinium species exhibit characteristic red-shifted fluorescence emissions. The AFPs were studied as both thin films and nanoscale (ca. 50 nm) particles. Both forms can detect PFAS at a minimum concentration of ∼1 ppb and a maximum concentration of ∼100 ppt. In addition, films and particles of AFPs showed similar fluorescence responses to aqueous PFAS in milliq, DI water, as well as well water.
